issues.description: Claims 'The top and bottom swap each time they cross.' This is false for a tangential touch (where the curves meet but the difference does not change sign) — there the orientation does NOT swap. A literal-minded learner who memorizes 'always swap at each crossing' will assign the wrong top/bottom on a sub-interval of a tangency problem. The danger is partly self-correcting because the same section also tells you to re-identify top-vs-bottom on each piece, and section 15 models the test-point check.
issues.fix: Reword to 'The top and bottom can swap when they cross, so never assume — re-check which curve is on top with a test point on every sub-interval.' Tie the splitting rule to sign changes of (f-g), not to the raw count of intersection points.

issues.description: States 'Top minus bottom — always.' in isolation, with no hint that which curve is on top can change across the interval. Heard alone this nudges a learner toward picking one ordering once and integrating straight through. It is repaired later (section 16 and the modeled test-point in section 15), so it does not by itself produce a durable false belief.
issues.fix: Append a half-sentence: 'whichever curve is higher on THIS piece — and on the AP exam that can change part-way across, so check.'

issues.description: Says average value is 'the height of the rectangle ... that has the same area as the region under the curve.' This equivalence only holds when f >= 0; for a function that dips below the axis, average value equals signed-area/width, not the literal geometric area under the curve. The example (x^2 >= 0) is safe, but the unqualified phrasing could mislead on a sign-changing function.
issues.fix: Add 'when f stays at or above the axis' or restate as 'the same SIGNED area (net area) as the integral.'

issues.description: Asserts 'The AP exam will ask you to identify that crossing point' as a guarantee. This is a meta/exam claim rather than a math error and does not create a false mathematical belief, but stated as certainty it overpromises.
issues.fix: Soften to 'The AP exam often asks you to find where the function equals its average value.'

notes: Every arithmetic example (x^2 avg=3, t^2-4 giving net -3 and total 23/3, x vs x^2 giving 1/6, the y-integral giving 9/2, x^2 vs 2x giving 4/3) checks out exactly, and displacement-vs-distance is cleanly and repeatedly separated; the only real misconception risk is the absolute 'swap each time they cross' / 'top minus bottom always' phrasing, which the video's own test-point procedure mostly self-corrects, so no flaw rises to a durable false belief.
